transcript: This is hot honey pork belly. We add some peppercorns, some bay leaves. While we wait for the pork belly to poach, we're gonna make our hot honey. Half a cup of hot honey and a fourth cup of rice vinegar. This is heated up to 400. Let it crisp up. So after 10 minutes, I'm taking the first fry out, and then I'm bringing the heat up to 450. And take this hot honey and brush it right onto the pork belly. Sweet with a crunch. Perfect.
vocabulary: pork belly, peppercorns, bay leaves, poach, is heated up to, crisp up, bring the heat up to, brush onto
grammarStructures: While we wait for...we're gonna...; I'm V-ing

transcript: Let’s make a skin beauty water. We’re going to start by adding warm water. Next thing we’re going to add is goji berries. Then we’re going to add half of a lemon which contains lots of vitamin C. And you really want to get everything out of your lemon so that your skin beauty water contains lots of vitamin C. Last thing we’re going to add is one kiwi, plus the peel of the kiwi, which is also packed with vitamin C. After adding the three ingredients, we’re going to mix everything together, so that it can really spread in the water. The most important part is to not only drink the water but also to eat everything that’s inside of the water.
vocabulary: goji berries: kỷ tử, is also packed with, spread in the water, get everything out of your lemon
grammarStructures: The most important part is to not only drink the water but also to eat everything that’s inside of the water.; to not only V but also to V

transcript:
I'm emptying those stinky trash cans and pouring hot water inside from my kettle.
Adding a disposable pad to this cleaning wand and scrubbing until it lathers, rinsing them off, wiping them down with the disposable microfiber towels, dropping in these air fresheners and reloading bags.

transcript:
So a time in my life where I was significantly more average than any other time in my life, like, I'm, everything was just average, was the point in my life and the times in my life when I was allowing perception of other people to influence my decision making.
So, like, literally, I would not post content like this because I was scared of what other people would think and how they would perceive me. I would not quit a job, I would not start a new job. I would not literally do anything because I was literally allowing those decisions and that decision making to be primarily influenced by what other people would think.
Feel like looking back now, the only thing that has, like, genuinely helped me so much with kind of cutting out all that perception is just continuous exposure therapy. Trying things and taking risks and honestly, publicly taking those risks and publicly failing also at the same time was, like, the best and most ultimate exposure therapy ever.
I'm just so focused on what I'm able to get done for me on a given day that I just, like, don't even think about it. But literally, seven years ago, the old version of me would be like, every single person was watching my every move which one is not true. No one cares about you that much. I'm so sorry. Like, nobody cares about you that much.
But two, even if people were talking, About me behind my back. Like, that's not my business. That's not my business.
What is my business? Is doing myself the service of being able to go after, literally whatever I want to go after.
In any given point in time. You can just decide one day to be like, oh, I'm so back, and I am so clocking in for my shift in the cringe factory and climbing Cringe Mountain and doing whatever those things are on any given day.
To expose yourself to the reality that, 1. No one cares, and 2, even if they do, it's none of your business.
vocabulary: a time in my life, cutting out all that perception, go after, clock in: the process of officially recording the start of a work shift
grammarStructures: I'm just so focused on what I'm able to get done for me on a given day

transcript:
Back then, I was afraid of studying abroad because there wasn’t enough information. Now, I’m afraid of it because there’s too much information. Back then, studying abroad could really change your life. Now, most people come back and nothing really changes. It’s like university. Before, getting a degree could change your life. Now, finishing university doesn’t guarantee anything. 
After working for a while, I realized something: those “big bosses” don’t really care who has a higher degree or a lower one. Everyone just plays a role in their system. What they care about is whether someone can do the job. Even cleaners, security guards, or the photocopy guy — if they leave, it becomes a real problem to replace them. And I’ve also noticed that people who aren’t highly educated often make more money. Because in their mind, the goal is simple: make money. They don’t have too many standards or expectations that slow them down.
Sometimes I feel like I’m pushing myself to rush through something… something that I’ll actually have to do for the rest of my life. Even if I rush and “finish” something, there’s no real end game anyway.
I don’t even think studying abroad is about “changing my life.” I just want to break all the assumptions in my head — to be able to explain, in a deeper way, everything that’s happening in my life. I also realize that I live a lot on the internet, so the culture I absorb is very different from real life. People like me — who grew up online — either have already reached that world, or are still trying to get there.
Sometimes I wonder why I keep trying to digitize everything from offline life. How much does a lab really need to be online? How much does teaching Chinese in Vietnam actually need to be online? Why is everything slowly moving online?
And then I think — maybe it’s not that people lack information. It’s that they need certain kinds of information. They are drawn to what they like.
Just because someone studies more or reaches more advanced knowledge doesn’t mean they are more “advanced” than someone farming in the countryside. They can bring technology to help farmers work more efficiently — but they don’t need to force farmers to become like them.
Even on the internet, everyone likes different kinds of content. If I push English, Chinese, or advanced knowledge onto someone who just enjoys funny animal videos, they’ll just see it as nonsense.
I’m on the internet, watching people from all over the world, because I like seeing the world that way. It’s not that the content influences me, or that I’m obsessed with the West and need to physically go there.
I am that content. I’m part of that world. I don’t need to go to that country — I just need to reach that content. But the people around me don’t like that kind of content. So I have to put it online — and that’s how I find people who are like me. And once I already have a community like that,
if I just sit in one place online, then I’m wasting it. 
That’s the contradiction. When I do something in real life, I want to turn it into something online. But when I stay online too much, I want to turn it into something real. It’s honestly confusing. Sometimes I don’t even understand what I’m thinking anymore.
grammarStructures: rush through; Why is everything slowly moving online?; push onto

transcript:
I feel like a lot of men truly don't understand that they are affected by the male loneliness epidemic and that it is kind of a vicious cycle. But they kind of treat it as, like, a little joke. And I just think so much of their lives are just strongly correlated to things that cause the loneliness. For example, if you go on a dating app, you will probably, You can swipe, like, five or 10 people, and there will be guaranteed at least a few of them that are going to mention that they love an Irish goodbye.
I don't know if this is a me thing or just the way that maybe I was raised culturally, but I could never fathom an Irish goodbye. I always say goodbye to all my friends and family before I leave. Like, whenever I leave the function, I have to say goodbye to everyone because it's the way that I was raised. And I can't fathom just being like, I'm just gonna dip I'm never gonna tell anyone. Nobody knows where I am, I'm gonna disappear, and everyone's fine with that.
Like, nobody's gonna check in. They're just gonna be like, oh, yeah, Jocelyn's gone. She's good. We're not gonna check in on her. We're not gonna try to figure out where she is. If I disappeared from a party or a gathering, you best believe someone would call 911. I know that for a fact. I know plenty of my friends would be so worried, and they would never get home. Like, they would never live with themselves if something went wrong, and they never did anything about it. So they would be searching for me. 
So then tie that to the phenomenon of young men going missing after a night out. It's becoming so common. This is becoming so common from just a regular night out, not even just big things like festivals and concerts, because that was happening before, but now it's happening more often with regular nights. Why is it so common for men to not look out for their friends? Do you guys not worry for each other? Do you guys really not care enough to be like, tell me when you get home, let me know that you're safe. Let me know where you are, share your location with me. All of my friends have my location, and I have their locations. Like, we check constantly check on each other. And there's probably inevitably gonna be someone in the comments going like, oh, it's not that serious, or, that's so gay. But literally, I feel like this extends into so many aspects of life, including life satisfaction, peace of mind. Almost like a life standard, a relationship standard, friendship standard.
Human connection is so important, and I think men need to start taking that very seriously.
vocabulary: Irish goodbye: the act of leaving without telling anyone, fathom /ˈfæð·əm/ to discover the meaning of something, dip
grammarStructures: And I just think so much of their lives are just strongly correlated to things that cause the loneliness.; there will be guaranteed at least a few of them that are going to...; whenever I leave the function; We're not gonna check in on her.; you best believe someone would call 911; I know plenty of my friends would be so worried, and they would never get home.; Like, they would never live with themselves if something went wrong, and they never did anything about it.; So they would be searching for me.; So then tie that to the phenomenon of...; this extends into so many aspects of life; And there's probably inevitably gonna be someone in the comments going like

transcript:
Something I didn't expect after getting laid off, my permanent neck, back, and shoulder pain that I've had my entire life is gone.
I've been unemployed for four weeks now and I noticed this about two weeks in. My back and neck pain. gone.
I'm talking multiple rounds of PT, years of professional massages, stretches, even a tech neck pillow. Nothing ever fixed the permanent knots that I had in my shoulder and back until I got laid off. And now no more pain.
Who woulda thunk not spending 8+ hours a day, 5 days a week sat in front of a desk with the posture of a shrimp would fix my back issues?
Not something I expected whatsoever, but this is literally the first time in my entire life I've not had back pain.
safe to say I'm enjoying this and I can't wait to find out what other perks funemployment has in store for me :)
grammarStructures: Who woulda thunk not V-ing would VO?; Not something I expected whatsoever, but...; what other perks N has in store for me

transcript:
How are religious women going to be anti abortion and then proceed to have premarital sex?
And then they either A. Shame other women for having an abortion or B. Have the kid that they never wanted. Because they're anti abortion as a result of their premarital sex and then pass on generational trauma to their child.
I'm sorry, but the math ain't math and the logic does not make sense here. 2 + 2 = 5. Like, I just. Maybe it's because I've never been in the state of religious psychosis, but it simply just does not compute in my head. 
Like, you would think that if you're against abortion you would either wait until marriage or just stay celibate until you find, quote unquote the one. But somehow you're having premarital sex, yet you're anti abortion and or having children you didn't even want and then putting your child through trauma.

transcript:
And there's no feeling better in life than when everyone at work hates the same person.
And look, I love talking shit with my best friends, and I love beating a dead horse with my best friends.
But talking shit about a co worker colleague you fucking hate at work with everyone else?
Bitch, there is no feeling more cathartic, more validating, more comradery inducing than talking shit about someone you hate with fellow colleagues.
When. Ugh. Because when money is on the line, the rants be even more feral.

transcript:
I'm a girl's girl, but this is a list of girls that I would respectfully put in a headlock if I ever met in real life.
No. 1, coming in strong at the top of our pyramid, we have homewreckers. Need I say more? If you know that a guy has a girl and you're still going for him, yeah, yeah, I'm putting you in a headlock. I'm throwing you over my shoulder.
Girls who pretend to be nice when they're really not. Like I love bitches, I love mean, bitchy, attitudey girls, but as long as you're just true to that essence, we're good. Like I rather you be bitchy to my face than you talking shit behind my back.
No. 3, we have girls that treat you differently around guys and emphasis on the word treat because I don't mind girls that act differently around guys cause that's pretty common and sometimes it's genuinely just a means of validation for them and I really don't mind that but I draw the line at when they treat you differently around guys like when they become meaner to you and when they just mistreat you. Yeah, no baby girl. That's where I draw the line.
This might be a controversial take and I might get canceled for this, but girls who call other girls pick me. I don't know why, but I just find the girls who call other girls pick me's are usually the pick me's like why do You have so much hatred in your heart that you must also then pull down another girl, even if she is already going low. When she is already low, you do not go lower, baby. You go higher because that's who you are.
Last but not least, we have girls who say, you're copying me. Like baby girl, baby girl. Let me see you do that. Like baby girl. We do not copy over here. We do not gate keep. There's no competition here, babe.
If you are triggered by this list, then maybe you should do some inner work. And if you don't do any of the things on this list, then stay the baddie that you already are.
vocabulary: put in a headlock: To restrain someone by wrapping one's arm around someone's head or neck, coming in strong, attitudey: Showing a pointedly arrogant attitude, essence: the basic or most important idea or quality of something, genuinely  /ˈdʒen.ju.ɪn.li/: really and sincerely,  just a means of validation, pull down another girl,
grammarStructures: Like I rather you be adj than you Ving; draw the line at when...; she is already Ving;
